data.title: EVs will overtake gas-powered cars as market dynamics shift dramatically
data.description:
Internal combustion engines (or ICEs) are approaching obsolescence despite media narratives suggesting electric vehicles are the failing technology, according to a new analysis from Ark Investments. 

Market share data for 2024 revealed a dramatic consumer shift away from traditional gas-powered vehicles, with ICE market share declining by approximately 5 percentage points annually since 2019.

“This trend is expected to accelerate as battery electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids continue gaining market traction,” said Akaash TK, research associate of Autonomous Technology and Robotics at Ark Investments.

While plug-in hybrid electric vehicles have recently experienced significant relative growth, battery electric vehicles have shown faster overall growth throughout the past decade, he said.

Traditional automakers are leveraging plug-in hybrid EVs (or PHEVs) as a transitional strategy while they struggle to produce competitive, affordable fully electric vehicles, according to Ark Investments. 

“Innovative competitors capitalizing on declining battery costs are positioned to introduce much less expensive BEVs (battery electric vehicles),” TK said, and added that the potential emergence of commoditized robotaxi fleets could further accelerate ICE vehicle decline.

China offers a revealing preview of this global transition, with BEVs and PHEVs capturing approximately 25% and 19% market share respectively last year.

This pushed China’s ICE share down to 52%, the analysis showed, significantly below the 80% ICE market share observed outside China.

Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) announced plans to introduce a lower-cost electric vehicle model could substantially boost global EV demand beyond China, according to Ark Investments.

“Despite recent tariff developments introducing uncertainty, BEVs are positioned to become the most performant and lowest-priced transportation solution globally,” TK concluded.

data.title: Shopify Q1 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) is set to post first quarter results on Thursday, before markets open.

Wall Street expects the company to post EPS of $0.26, while revenue is expected to rise 25% to $2.33 billion during the quarter.

The Canadian company saw higher sales as a strong consumer spending and its investment on AI tools to attract customers paid off.

Shopify’s fourth quarter revenue shot up 31.3% year-over-year in Q4 to $2.81 billion to accelerate from the 24% growth seen for the same quarter last year. The quarter marked the e-commerce company's seventh consecutive quarter of 25% or greater revenue growth when excluding logistics.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings are cautious and rated the stock a Hold. However, Wall Street analysts rated it a Buy.

BMO Capital Markets analysts see the e-commerce company as uniquely positioned to withstand tariff pressures in comparison to peers, and having substantial runway for growth on several fronts.

Given its spending on AI, investors would also be interested to know where the company’s margins are headed.

“Revenue growth and GAAP EPS will be of lower importance of investors as opposed to some other profitability metrics that will give clues regarding Shopify's operational performance as the company expands its ecosystem,” noted Seeking Alpha analyst Vladimir Dimitrov.

Over the last two years, Shopify has beaten both revenue and EPS estimates 100% of the time. Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 7 upward revisions, compared to 19 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have been revised upwards 25 times versus 10 downward moves.

The stock lost over 10% so far this year, compared to the near 5% fall in the broader S&P500 Index.

data.title: Bunge maintains 2025 EPS guidance at $7.75 amid focus on strategic divestitures and acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bunge Global SA (BG) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Greg Heckman highlighted the company’s strong start to 2025, driven by adaptability and execution within volatile markets. He emphasized the planned combination with Viterra, describing it as a strategic move to enhance global supply chain resilience, although regulatory approvals are still pending.
  • Recent divestitures of the European Margarines and Spreads business and the North American corn milling business were noted as aligning the portfolio with global value chains.
  • The company exceeded Q1 expectations due to activity pull forward from Q2, influenced by trade dynamics and regulatory uncertainties, prompting proactive moves by farmers and consumers.
  • CFO John Neppl reported Q1 adjusted EPS of $1.81, down from $3.04 year-over-year, and adjusted segment EBIT of $406 million, reflecting lower processing results in North America and Europe. He detailed reductions in costs, including a $45 million decrease in net interest expense.
  • Neppl highlighted a $392 million adjusted funds from operations in Q1, with $338 million in discretionary cash flow after sustaining CapEx, and $300 million in retained cash flow.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance of approximately $7.75, excluding impact from announced acquisitions and divestitures.
  • Agribusiness results are expected to be slightly lower due to reduced processing margins, while results in milling are projected to improve year-over-year.
  • Adjusted annual effective tax rate is estimated between 21% and 25%, with net interest expense expected to range from $220 million to $250 million.

Financial Results

  • First quarter adjusted EPS was $1.81, with adjusted EBIT at $406 million. Reported EPS was $1.48, impacted by notable items related to Viterra transaction costs.
  • Refined and Specialty Oils experienced reduced results in most regions, attributed to a balanced supply-demand environment and U.S. biofuel policy uncertainties.
  • Milling results declined due to competitive pricing pressures in South America, despite slight improvements in North America.
  • Adjusted leverage ratio at the end of Q1 stood at 0.6x, supported by $8.7 billion in unused committed credit facilities and a $3.2 billion cash balance.

Q&A

  • Salvatore Tiano, Bank of America: Asked about the Viterra acquisition's regulatory hurdles and the termination of the CJ Selecta transaction. CEO Heckman reiterated confidence in Viterra’s approval and cited regulatory delays for ending the CJ Selecta deal.
  • Tom Palmer, Citi: Queried on earnings cadence and assumptions for biofuel policy clarity. CFO Neppl estimated a 60-40 split in earnings between H1 and H2, maintaining guidance despite uncertainties.
  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Sought clarification on the partnership with Repsol. Management emphasized the strategic alignment with Repsol to address lower carbon fuel demands in Europe.
  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research: Inquired about processing margins and tariff impacts. CEO Heckman noted margin improvements in Europe and Asia and highlighted the flexibility of their global footprint to adapt to trade policy changes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative, with concerns around regulatory delays, biofuel policy uncertainties, and weaker Q1 results compared to past periods.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing adaptability and long-term strategic alignment. However, responses in the Q&A were more defensive, particularly regarding regulatory issues and transaction terminations.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady, with continued optimism about strategic initiatives and operational resilience.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remained consistent at $7.75 adjusted EPS for 2025, with similar expectations for agribusiness and refined oil performance compared to last quarter.
  • Analysts continued to focus on regulatory and biofuel policy challenges, mirroring prior concerns about market volatility and geopolitical factors.
  • Management highlighted progress in divestitures and partnerships, contrasting with prior discussions focused on acquisition closures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Regulatory delays for the Viterra acquisition remain a significant concern, with management citing ongoing constructive discussions with Chinese authorities.
  • Weaker U.S. biofuel policy clarity could impact refining premiums and overall margins.
  • Tariff uncertainties and their effects on global trade flows pose risks to operational flexibility and profitability.

Final Takeaway

Bunge Global SA delivered a strong start to 2025, with Q1 results exceeding expectations due to strategic moves and market adaptability. Management reaffirmed confidence in achieving the $7.75 full-year EPS guidance despite challenges such as regulatory delays, biofuel policy uncertainties, and tariff impacts. Strategic divestitures and partnerships, including the pending Viterra acquisition, reinforce the company’s focus on optimizing its global footprint and enhancing long-term resilience.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: SMCI, MRVL, UPST, NVO, DIS, and more
data.description:
Stock futures climbed Wednesday following confirmation of renewed trade talks between the U.S. and China, fueling optimism over easing tensions between the world’s two largest economies. Investors also looked ahead to the Federal Reserve’s interest rate decision due later in the day.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Electronic Arts (NASDAQ:EA) +6% – Shares advanced after the company topped core FQ4 metrics and issued an upbeat FY2026 net bookings outlook. EA guided FY2026 net bookings to $7.6B–$8.0B (midpoint $7.8B) vs. $7.62B consensus, driven by strength in EA SPORTS, The Sims, and the upcoming launches of Battlefield and Skate, partly offset by ~5 pts of expected softness in catalog titles and Apex Legends. For Q1, EA projects net bookings of $1.175B–$1.275B (midpoint $1.225B) vs. $1.275B consensus.
  • Opendoor Technologies (NASDAQ:OPEN) +10% – Shares jumped after the company posted better-than-expected Q1 results and issued upbeat Q2 guidance. The online real estate platform expects Q2 revenue of $1.45B–$1.53B (midpoint $1.49B), topping the $1.45B consensus. It also guided contribution profit of $65M–$75M (vs. $73.6M Visible Alpha consensus) and adjusted EBITDA of $10M–$20M, well above the estimated -$11.2M.
  • Novo Nordisk (NVO) +8% — Shares grew after Q1 sales climbed 19% Y/Y, led by a 21% increase in diabetes and obesity care, with Wegovy sales surging 83%. U.S. sales rose 20%, and management expects Wegovy availability to improve in H2 as compounded GLP-1 drugs are phased out. Despite strong growth, Q1 Wegovy sales missed expectations, and the company lowered its 2025 guidance due to slower-than-expected penetration of branded GLP-1 therapies in the U.S. Novo now expects 2025 sales growth of 13–21% (vs. a prior 18–26%) and operating profit growth of 16–24% at CER (vs. a prior 19–27%), with FX seen cutting reported growth by 3–5 pts.
  • Disney (NYSE:DIS) +10% – Shares soared after the company posted a double beat for FQ2 and issued strong full-year guidance. FY2025 adjusted EPS is expected at $5.75, above the $5.44 consensus, and operating cash flow at $17B (vs. $14.8B consensus). Disney+ added more subscribers than expected, with modest sequential growth guided for the current quarter. The company also announced plans for its seventh theme park resort in Abu Dhabi, in partnership with Miral on Yas Island.
  • Charles River Laboratories (NYSE:CRL) +19% – Shares surged after the company beat Q1 estimates and raised its full-year guidance. The contract research firm now expects full-year revenue to decline (5.5)% to (3.5)% vs. prior outlook of (7.0)% to (4.5)% and (~6%) consensus. Adjusted EPS guidance was raised to $9.30–$9.80, up from $9.10–$9.60 and in line with the $9.31 consensus.

Biggest stock losers

  • Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) -5% – Shares fell after the company reported mixed FQ3 results and lowered FY2025 revenue guidance to $21.8B–$22.6B from $23.5B–$25B, falling short of the $23.5B consensus. Q4 revenue is expected at $5.6B–$6.4B, much below the $6.81B consensus, with non-GAAP EPS projected at $0.40–$0.50, also below the $0.60 consensus.
  • Upstart Holdings (NASDAQ:UPST) -15% – Shares tumbled after Q2 revenue guidance of ~$225M missed the $226M consensus, while the full-year outlook, though slightly raised, aligned with expectations. FY2025 revenue is now forecast at ~$1.01B, including $920M in fee income and $90M in net interest income, up from the prior $80M estimate. Adjusted EBITDA margin is projected at ~19%, slightly below the 20.49% consensus. Q1 results topped estimates, but soft guidance weighed on market sentiment.
  • Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) -17% – Shares plunged after the company reported mixed Q1 results and slashed its FY2025 revenue outlook to $2.3B–$2.6B (vs. $2.9B–$3.1B prior and $3.05B consensus), citing a revised outlook for Elevidys. Sarepta also raised its full-year non-GAAP R&D and SG&A expense guidance to $1.78B–$2.18B from $1.2B–$1.3B, driven by Arrowhead collaboration costs and potential DM1 milestone payments.
  • Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) -10% – Shares plummeted after the company postponed its June 2025 investor day to 2026, citing macro uncertainty. Marvell also narrowed its Q1 FY2026 revenue guidance to ~$1.875B ±2% (from ±5%). Following the announcement, Cantor Fitzgerald downgraded the stock to Neutral (from Overweight) and slashed the price target to $60 (from $125), citing potential ASIC losses tied to AMZN and MSFT, with Microsoft possibly shifting to Broadcom chips in future generations.

data.title: Westwater Resources gets LOI from Export-Import Bank for Kellyton graphite plant
data.description:
Westwater Resources (NYSE:WWR) +3.2% in early trading Wednesday after saying it received a letter of interest from the U.S. Export-Import Bank for the Kellyton graphite processing plant in Alabama, under EXIM's Make More in America Initiative and China and Transformational Export Program.

"This LOI from EXIM adds additional potential sources of funding as we continue the development of our Kellyton graphite anode processing plant, which is 100% sold out for phase one," Westwater (NYSE:WWR) CFO Steve Cates said.

The letter of interest is separate from the previously announced Phase 1 debt syndication process for the Kellyton plant, which is still progressing.

The Kellyton graphite plant is under construction in Alabama; Westwater's (WWR) nearby Coosa graphite deposit is the largest and most advanced natural flake graphite deposit in the contiguous U.S.
